<!-- do not edit; create revised prose in manuscript-draft/ -->

## IX. Never First

She does not let herself decide he is the author of all five. That is the newspapers' arithmetic, and she has no way to do it, and she knows the difference between what she can count and what she can't — her whole life has been kept in ledgers that had to balance. And she does not let herself do the other thing either, the thing the banked heat under her ribs has wanted since the thirtieth of September: go and find him in the dark and be sure.

Because those are the rules, and she keeps them the way her mother keeps fast days — resentfully, completely. Never first: he must be moving before she is, and it is not her place to make him move. Never in anger: and here she does the hardest work of her life, harder than any stance her father ever held her in, because the heat wants this — wants it to be him, wants it to be for Kate and for Liz, and, lower down and older, for Lee, which is the entry she scratches out first, every time, because Lee's debt is real, and owed, and exactly the kind the rules forbid her to collect — and the rules say that whoever goes looking for a debt in an alley will collect it whether it is owed or not. So she does not go looking. She takes the heat and banks it and goes back to folding shirts, which is the whole of what the practice asks of her now: to do nothing, correctly, for as long as nothing is what the rules require. It is the hardest thing she has ever not done.

She keeps back only one small thing for herself. Since the killings she has taken to carrying her grandfather's fan in her sleeve — the plain folded wooden one that has corrected her elbows all her life, its wood warm against the inside of her wrist within a minute of leaving the drawer — not as a plan and not as a hunt, but for the same reason her father has taken to standing in his doorway at closing with his hands folded where the street can see them: a private steadiness, the one piece of the dawn yard small enough to carry into a frightening city. She tells herself it means nothing. She is nineteen, and it is that kind of autumn, and every woman on the dock road is carrying something now.

Twice in the weeks after the shirt she feels it on the walk between shop and bar — not footsteps, nothing so generous as footsteps: the particular pressure on the back of the neck that the yard taught her never to argue with. Both times she stops at a window as if minding her reflection, and reads the street behind her in the dark glass, and both times the street is only a street. It fits. It proves nothing. By November she has understood that this sentence is the whole shape of her life now, and will go on being it until one of them makes a mistake.

She is not looking for him at all, on the night the fog puts him in front of her.

* * *
